131 - You Are Losing Sales From THIS OBJECTION - YouTube.mp4
How do you respond when the prospect says, 'We really like what you're offering, but we did some research and we found a lot of negative reviews about your company online?' How many sales do you lose every single week, every single month, every single year, just from that one objection? Because every single company we all know will have negative reviews. There is no company on planet earth that does not have at least one crazy negative review from some crazy person. It just is what it is. You know, I love Range Rovers, but if I Google them right now, I'm probably going to see some negative reviews. Now, mainly positive, but for sure there's going to be a few negative reviews. So how do you help them overcome that?
And then how do you help your prospects overcome the objection of 'this sounds too good to be true'? Okay. How many of you have lost sales when a prospect says, 'I really like this,' but it just sounds too good to be true? And so they just back out. Okay. Now, if you are brand new to our Facebook group, you've never seen me before. You're brand new to TikTok here because we've only started on TikTok about six weeks ago, or if you just started following me here on IG, you don't know who the hell I am. I am the chairman and founder of a company called Seventh Level, all right? So we're a training— sales training, obviously a global sales training company— and we train people exactly like you watching me right now.
So we train sales professionals exactly like you. We train. management and managers like you, we train sales executives and leaders like you. We train businesses, companies, entrepreneurs, coaches, and consultants like you. Anybody who has a product or service that they want to sell more of. And we train you by transforming the way you and your team sell by learning specific skilled questions and techniques to get this. Actually work with human behavior. Actually work with human behavior— do you actually know what that means? Okay, rather than work against it, right? We don't want to use techniques that work against human behavior, that trigger prospects to shut down, emotion that trigger objections, that trigger our prospects to go into fight or flight mode. Now, this, this methodology is known as N E P Q.
It stands for neuro-emotional persuasion questioning. We teach you with the right tonality, the right delivery of the questions, because you just can't sound like a scripted rogue. That puts your prospects at ease, it eliminates sales pressure. Can you imagine, if the sales pressure, the sales resistance was gone, and it triggers your prospects to actually want to engage, want to open up to, because they feel like you might have something important to them. So we have to first find out what do they actually mean when they say 'we saw a bunch of negative reviews online'? How many did they see? So let me role play with myself. I'm going to show you how to easily resolve this. Help them resolve it. This is one of the most easiest objections in the world to help your prospect overcome. If you lose a sale, from this. You just haven't learned human behavior yet, okay? Prospects says, 'You know, we're doing some research over the weekend. We saw your company has some bad reviews. You simply ask an NEPQ clarifying question first.
How do you mean by bad reviews? So that's the first question. How do you mean by bad reviews? That's the first question you ask. That simple. It's an NAPQ clarifying question. When you say bad reviews, what do you mean exactly? Or how do you mean by bad reviews? Then the prospect says something like this. Well, I mean, it's not that bad. I mean, there were some people saying some bad things about your company. And then you ask.' I mean, were these... Were these actual clients? Were these actual clients or just some negative people online who were never clients? That's the first question you ask. Were these actual clients or just some negative people online? Now, why would you ever ask that?
Because first of all, a lot of reviews, maybe you guys don't understand, a lot of reviews you get that your company gets are not actually from real clients. They might be from somebody that's a competitor. They might be from somebody on the outside that's just trying to take your company down. So you ask that simple question that is a real live question. And it gets the prospect to think. That maybe a lot of the reviews they saw weren't even from real clients, that they could be making a mistake. So it puts that seed of doubt in their mind that their reviews they heard, that they read, might not be from real clients. They might be making a mistake here. So were these, and you slow this down when you ask, because you're curious, and you say, 'were these...
I mean, were they actual clients or just some negative people online?' See my facial expressions, my tonality. Even if you're on the phone and they can't see you, you would still do that because that affects your tonality. It affects your body language. Okay. And that comes across in your tone. All right. Then they're going to say. Then they're going to say, 'Well, I mean, I think some were clients, but I'm not sure if they were all clients.' And then you're going to say, 'Now, when you say you saw some negative reviews. How many did you actually see?' Now, why would you want to ask them how many they saw? Because let's say that you sell for Range Rover and Range Rover has... 3.
9 million clients and there's 12 negative reviews. Well, what would that mean? That 99. 9999... 99% of the clients are happy. That's what that means, right? So we want to ask them, because when they say we saw negative reviews, does that mean they saw 10,000? Does that mean they saw three? Does that mean they saw 3,000? We don't know what that means. Okay. So here's what you want to ask. Now, when you saw, when you say you saw negative reviews, how many did you actually see? Okay. Now, let's pretend for a second that you sell for more of an SMB company. Okay. And let's say online, you've got hundreds of positive reviews, but let's say that you have 10 or 15 negative reviews.
So they say, they come back and they say, 'Well, You know, I didn't really count them up, but if I was guessing, I probably saw maybe 10 or 15.' Negative reviews. Then you're going to ask, 'Okay.' What is the math on there? That would be, get the calculator out, that would be about point. 0. 02%. So 0. 02%. So that would be about one fifth of 1%. So about one fifth, about one fifth of 1% that we're complaining. While our other clients that would leave 99. 8% have had great experiences and actually gotten the results they came to us for. My mom says I'm not the sharpest tool in the shed. So that would be. Okay. That would be 0. 02%. So that would be about one fifth of 1% that were complaining. While the other 99. 8% of clients have had great experiences and actually gotten results. So, if we have a 99% 8% success rate, what would that possibly mean about us getting our clients' results?
And then the prospect is: they're not going to argue. They're going to say, 'Well, yeah, it sounds like you guys do great things.' Then you would say, 'Well, yeah, I mean, our clients would say that for sure, but can I... Can I possibly make a suggestion to you?' Prospect says, 'Sure, go ahead.' Have you ever considered that no matter how great you do for your clients, that there will always be some people that, for whatever reason, will not do what they're supposed to do on their end. And then who did they? Who do they usually blame that on? And then the prospect is going to say, 'Well, yeah, they blame the company.' I mean, we have clients like that that complain. So, you know, thanks for clearing that up. That is the easiest objection in the world. To help them overcome. Hopefully, that helped you on that objection.
